Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

001
      A LIGNING L ARGE L ANGUAGE M ODELS WITH P REF -
002   ERENCE P RIVACY
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006    Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                               A BSTRACT
010
011            Alignment is a crucial part in the implementation pipeline of Large Language
012            Models (LLMs) that utilizes human feedback to ensure that LLMs adhere to hu-
013            man values and societal norms. This introduces privacy threats associated with the
014            identity and preferences of the labelers responsible for creating the human feed-
015            back data. Several recent works have explored using differential privacy (DP) as a
016            notion to protect the privacy of human labeled data; primarily relying on DP-SGD
017            based solutions, which privatize the gradients during fine-tuning and alignment.
018            Human preferences, however are only associated with the labels of the (prompt,
019
               response) tuples; therefore DP-SGD based approaches can be superfluous, provid-
               ing more privacy than necessary and can degrade model utility. In this work, we
020
               focus on the problem of aligning LLMs with preference level privacy, which only
021
               preserve the privacy of preferences provided by humans. We build and expand
022            upon the concept of label DP for this problem, and present a series of increasingly
023            sophisticated, yet practical privacy preserving mechanisms for alignment. Specif-
024            ically, starting from a standard randomized response (RR) mechanism which ran-
025            domly flips human preferences, and its corresponding unbiased RR mechanism
026            (which ensures an unbiased loss during alignment), we propose a new mechanism,
027            PROPS (PROgressively Private Self-alignment). PROPS works in multiple stages
028            as follows: in each stage, the privately trained and partially aligned model from the
029            previous stage to act as a labeler for the training data for the next stage and com-
030
               bine it with RR which is repeated across multiple stages. Motivation for PROPS
               comes from the following critical observations: a) learning to label correct pref-
031
               erences might be an easier problem than generating responsible content; b) pro-
032
               gressively combining RR with partially aligned models for labeling preferences
033            significantly reduces the amount of necessary perturbation needed for privacy and
034            also shows the potential of possibly reducing the number of human labeled pref-
035            erence samples. We present comprehensive experiments using multiple models
036            (including Pythia and GPT models), and two datasets (truthy-dpo-v0.1 and
037            Anthropic HH-RLHF) to showcase the utility gains of PROPS over existing pri-
038            vacy preserving methods (including DP-SGD and RR). Our results demonstrate
039            that PROPS is especially effective in high-privacy regimes compared to conven-
040            tional DP-SGD. For example, with ϵ = 0.1 the Win-Tie-Lose rates of PROPS
041
               against DP-SGD for GPT2-Large and GPT2-Medium are respectively 66 : 2 : 32
               and 76 : 1 : 23, which demonstrate a clear advantage of using PROPS.
042
043
044   1   I NTRODUCTION
045
      In recent years, Large Language Models (LLMs) have gained significant attention across various dis-
046
      ciplines due to their ability to generate responses based on open-ended human instructions. Training
047
      LLMs typically involves three key stages: pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and align-
048   ment. Among these, alignment is particularly important as it guides LLMs to produce responses
049   that align with societal norms and human preferences. The alignment process typically relies on a
050   dataset D consisting of n samples, each containing a prompt x, LLM-generated responses (y1 , y2 ),
051   and a human-preferred label ℓ∗ , collectively referred to as preference data (which one of y1 or y2
052   is the prefered response). Over the past few years, two main alignment approaches have emerged,
053   namely, Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) (Stiennon et al., 2020) and Direct
      Preference Optimization (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2024), both of which utilize preference datasets


                                                        1
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


054
      with ranked labels provided by human annotators. While alignment improves the quality of gener-
055   ated responses, the use of human-labeled samples introduces privacy risks, particularly concerning
056   the identity of the annotators and their associated preferences. Intuitively, LLM generated (prompt,
057   response) pairs in datasets may not require strict privacy protections, but human preference labels
058   require privacy, as they often reveal sensitive insights. For instance, in medical applications, pub-
059   licly available case reports or anonymized symptoms can prompt LLMs to generate diagnoses, with
060   human feedback providing expert alignment with best practices, necessitating privacy to safeguard
061   clinical judgments. Similarly, in policy analysis, publicly available survey questions or proposals
062   can elicit LLM-generated analyses, where policymakers’ feedback reveals sensitive interpretative
063
      insights that may require protection.
064   Related works & motivation: To mitigate the privacy
065   risks associated with human-annotated preference data,
066   the notion of Differential Privacy (DP) (Dwork et al.,
067   2014) has recently been explored for fine-tuning and
068   alignment of LLMs. For example, Yu et al. (2021) ap-
069
      plied DP to fine-tuning by introducing privacy guaran-
      tees for smaller, appended parameters such as LoRA and
070
      adapters. Behnia et al. (2022) proposed a DP fine-tuning
071
      framework for LLMs using the Edgesworth accountant, Figure 1: Comparison of Win-Tie-Lose
072   while Zheng et al. (2024) provided DP guarantees for in- rate for PROPS vs DP-SGD for high privacy
073   context learning. Singh et al. (2024) introduced a two- regimes with GPT2-Large, GPT2-Medium
074   stage fine-tuning process, and Yu et al. (2024) addressed models (for more results see Section 4).
075   the privacy-preserving alignment challenge by ensuring
076   DP protection for users’ prompts against labelers during the generation of preference datasets for
077   alignment. DP for LLM inference privacy has also been studied by Mai et al. (2024) and Flemings
078   et al. (2024). Additionally, Wu et al. (2023) proposed applying DP to RLHF by splitting the dataset
079   into three disjoint sets to ensure DP at each stage of RLHF. We now make the following critical
080
      observations that motivate this paper:
081
082         1. The aforementioned works view the entire tuple of {prompt = (x) , responses = (y1 , y2 ),
083
               human-annotated labels = (ℓ∗ )} as a private entry in the training dataset and the associated
               DP notion is invoked to protect this entire tuple. We note that in typical alignment scenar-
084
               ios, the prompts and responses are not generated by humans (the two responses (y1 , y2 ) are
085
               usually obtained from the fine-tuned LLM). Human input is only used to label/rank the re-
086            sponses. Therefore, providing privacy of the entire tuple can be superfluous and potentially
087            hurt the utility of the privately aligned model. This observation motivates the problem of
088            this paper: alignment with preference privacy, where only the preferences of the labelers
089            need to be protected, rather than prompt and response pairs.
090
091         2. Most of the existing works listed above achieve DP by using DP-Stochastic Gradient De-
092            scent (DP-SGD) and their variants, such as Differentially Private Proximal Policy Opti-
093            mization (DP-PPO). These approaches modify the training procedure by privatizing the
094            gradients based on the leakage budgets. While these methods are adequate in terms of
095
               privacy, they provide the privacy for the entire tuple (i.e., lack the specificity of only keep-
               ing human preferences private) and often degrade performance in high-privacy regimes (as
096
               shown in Figure 1).
097
098
099
      Overview and Main Contribution: Motivated by the above observations, we study the problem
      of aligning LLMs with preference privacy. Specifically, we investigate two notions of privacy:
100
      a) preference-level privacy and b) a stronger notion of labeler-level privacy. Preference level pri-
101
      vacy ensures that the individual human-preference ℓ∗ for any tuple (x, y1 , y2 ) does not significantly
102   impact the aligned model. Formally, we leverage the existing notion of Label-DP, and use it to
103   formalize the notion of (ϵ, δ)-preference-level DP, where (ϵ, δ) represent the privacy budgets. The
104   stronger notion of labeler-level privacy (also commonly referred to as “user” level privacy in the
105   DP literature) hides the presence/absence of any individual human labeler and protects all the labels
106   annotated by the labeler. For the scope of this paper, we focus predominantly on DPO Rafailov
107   et al. (2024) as the alignment algorithm for two reasons: a) there is no existing work on DPO with
      differential privacy; and b) DPO is less computationally expensive than RLHF, since DPO learns a

120
121
122   Figure 2: PROPS: Progressively Private Self-Alignment algorithm: PROPS aligns in multiple stages, where
123   in each stage, the model learned from the previous stage is used to generate labels/preferences for the next
124   batch; these preferences together with the noisy versions (perturbed via randomized response (RR)) of ground
      truth preferences are combined via a maximum-likelihood estimator (MLE) which are used for alignment.
125
126
      aligned model directly, whereas RLHF involves training a reward model, followed by reinforcement
127   learning stage. We summarize and highlight the main contributions and novel aspects of this work:
128
129          • Randomized Response base methods: We start by studying the classical mechanism of
130            Randomized response (RR) which directly perturbs the human-labels, which can then be
131            used for model alignment via conventional DPO training. This method however, ends up
132            introducing a bias in the loss.
133          • PROPS Algorithm: we present Progressively Private Self Alignment (PROPS), a novel al-
134            gorithm which takes a more nuanced approach to alignment through a multi-staged process.
135            Rather than using the entire perturbed dataset in one go, PROPS divides the alignment task
136            into multiple stages, each involving a fraction of the dataset (see Figure 2). Let us focus on
137            the kth stage for k > 1: at this stage we have a model obtained from the previous stage,
138
               denoted by Mk−1 . We now make two observations: in the kth stage in order to use fresh
               batch of samples, we still need to perturb the true private labels (say using RR). However,
139
               we also have access to the model Mk−1 (which can be used without additional leakage due
140
               to post-processing of DP), as well as the non-private (prompts, responses) of the fresh batch
141            of samples. We use the model Mk−1 to also provide it’s own ranking of these responses
142            (and denote it as ℓMk−1 ). We also have the noisy perturbed labels, ℓRR on the fresh batch
143            of samples. Viewing (ℓRR , ℓMk−1 ) as two noisy observations of the ground truth pref-
144            erence ℓ∗ , we combine these observations and derive the maximum-likelihood estimator
145            (MLE) using both the observations. These MLE estimates act as the labels for this stage
146            of alignment. This process then repeats across K stages. The key insight behind PROPS is
147            that while intermediate LLM models Mk may not yet be fully capable of generating high-
148            quality outputs or responses in the early stages of training, it may still possess sufficient
149
               knowledge to correctly label preferences. By utilizing progressively learned models like
               for preference labeling, PROPS reduces the reliance on noisy perturbed labels, which can
150
               degrade the quality of alignment if overused. This staged approach not only improves the
151
               quality of the alignment over time but also lessens the burden of maintaining strict privacy
152            guarantees across the entire dataset. Each stage builds upon the knowledge accumulated
153            in previous stages, allowing for more refined preference labeling as the model improves.
154            Thus, our framework leverages the power of intermediate models to enhance alignment
155            efficiency while preserving privacy, offering a novel solution to the challenge of privacy-
156            preserving alignment. We remark here that PROPS can be also used/readily adopted for
157            RLHF based alignment.
158          • Comprehensive Experimental Results: We conduct a comprehensive set of experiments to
159            evaluate the impact of preference-level differential privacy (DP) on alignment performance
160            with different models (namely Pythia-1B, GPT2-Large and GPT2-Medium) across various
161            privacy regimes. Specifically, we focus on DPO-based methods for alignment and analyze
               how the Win-Tie-Lose rates of privately aligned DPO models vary as a function of the

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
               privacy budget ϵ. First, we compare Randomized Response (RR) based DPO against SFT
163            model to show the benefit of incorporating privacy during alignment. Second, we com-
164            pare the Win-Tie-Lose rates of our proposed method against conventional DP-SGD that
165            shows the benefit of using preference-level privacy over centralized differential privacy for
166            alignment. In Figure 1, we highlight results demonstrating that in high-privacy regimes, the
167            preference-privacy-based PROPS approach outperforms DP-SGD for two different models:
168            GPT2-Large and GPT2-Medium. We also analyze the effect of model size and privacy con-
169            straints when using PROPS compared to RR-DPO, and show that the gains from PROPS
170            are prominent for larger model sizes. We present the Win-Tie-Lose rate for two differ-
171
               ent datasets (truthy-dpo-v0.1 and Anthropic HH-RLHF) on GPT2-Medium model
               which show the consistent improvement provided by PROPS. The detailed experimental
172
               results along with discussions are provided in Section 4.
173
174   2    BACKGROUND ON ALIGNMENT AND PRIVACY
175
176   Model Alignment: Alignment of pre-trained LLM models enable the models adhere to human
177   values and societial norms by adjusting the generated responses. Reinforcement Learning based
178   Human Feedback (RLHF) (Stiennon et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2022) and Direct Preference Optimiza-
179   tion (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2024) are two pre-dominant methods for alignment. In this paper, we
180   focus on the DPO-based method for alignment. We start with a preference dataset D with n-samples
181   where the ith sample can be expressed as (xi , y1i , y2i , ℓ∗i ) where xi is the prompt, y1i , y2i are two LLM
182   generated responses and ℓ∗i is the human chosen label that can be defined as:
                                      1 if y1i is preferred over the response y2i ,

183                           ∗
184
                             ℓi =
                                      0 otherwise.
185
      For the ease of notation, we define yp is the preferred response and ynp is the not-preferred response,
186
      and suppress the index i. Specifically, if ℓ∗ = 1, then we will have yp = y1 , ynp = y2 , and for the
187   case ℓ∗ = 0, yp = y2 , ynp = y1 . For a prompt x in the dataset D with yp response preferred over
188   the response ynp , we define the DPO (instance specific) loss as:
189
                                                         πθ (yp |x)           πθ (ynp |x)

190           loss∗ (x, yp ≻ ynp ) = log σ β log                      − β log
191
                                                         πref (yp |x)         πref (ynp |x)
192                                  = 1(ℓ∗ = 1) · loss(x, y1 ≻ y2 ) + 1(ℓ∗ = 0) · loss(x, y2 ≻ y1 ).         (1)
193   The instant-specific true loss mentioned in equation 1 represents the loss for every prompt x in
194   preference data D, therefore the expected DPO loss can be defined as:
195   E[loss(x, y1 , y2 , ℓ∗ )] = E(x,y1 ,y2 ,ℓ∗ )∼D [1(ℓ∗ = 1) · loss(x, y1 ≻ y2 ) + 1(ℓ∗ = 0) · loss(x, y2 ≻ y1 )]
196                                                                                                           (2)
197   Privacy for Alignment: The notion of Differential Privacy (DP) Dwork et al. (2014) has been
198   adopted in the alignment frameworks to ensure that the presence or absence of a single sample
199   in a preference dataset does not significantly alter the outcome of the model.
200
201   Definition 1 ((ϵ, δ) Differential Privacy) For all pair of neighboring datasets D and D′ that differ
202   by a single entry, i.e., ||D − D′ ||1 ≤ 1, a randomized algorithm M with an input domain of D and
203
      output range R is considered to be (ϵ, δ)-differentially private, if ∀S ⊆ R:
204                                  P[M(D) ∈ S] ≤ eϵ · P[M(D′ ) ∈ S] + δ.
205
206
      The DP framework ensures that the inclusion or exclusion of a single data entry {xi , y1i , y2i , ℓ∗i } does
      not significantly influence the aligned model’s behavior. However, the traditional DP framework
207
      may offer a stronger privacy guarantee than what is required since it not only privatize the human
208
      generated label ℓ∗i in the context of LLM alignment that can be achieved by exploiting the idea of
209   Label-DP (Chaudhuri & Hsu, 2011; Ghazi et al., 2021b), a relaxed version of DP which specifically
210   aims to protect the privacy of the labels of the dataset, may offer a more ideal solution.
211
212   Definition 2 (ϵ, δ-Label DP) (Chaudhuri & Hsu, 2011; Ghazi et al., 2021a) For all neighbor-
213   ing datasets D and D′ that differ by one preference ranking (i.e. {xi , y1i , y2i , ℓi } ∈ D and
214   {xi , y1i , yi2 , (1 − ℓi )} ∈ D′ , with privacy parameters ϵ ∈ R>0 , and δ ∈ [0, 1) an algorithm M ,
215
      whose output domain S consists of all possibly aligned models, will satisfy ϵ-preference privacy if :
                                       P[M (D) ∈ S] ≤ eϵ · P[M (D′ ) ∈ S] + δ.                                   (3)


                                                            4
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


216
      For the case with δ = 0, the model M satisfies ϵ-label DP.This motivates the notion of Preference-
217   level privacy that places a guarantee on labeler’s privacy while not significantly impacting a model
218   during its alignment stage. Specifically, preference level privacy ensures that the resulting LLM after
219   alignment should not be significantly impacted by a change in a single preference. Similar to Label-
220   DP, the randomized response mechanism (Warner, 1965) is a direct approach for implementing
                                                                                                    1
221   preference privacy, as the preference ℓi of a data entry is flipped with probability γϵ = 1+e   ϵ , where

222   γϵ is used for convenience in notation and ϵ represents the privacy budget. We define ϵ-preference
223   level DP and the randomized response mechanism as follows:
224
225   Definition 3 ((ϵ, δ)-Preference level DP) For all neighboring datasets D and D′ that differ by one
226
      preference ranking (i.e. {xi , y1i , y2i , ℓi } ∈ D and {xi , y1i , yi2 , (1 − ℓi )} ∈ D′ , a model after perform-
      ing an alignment procedure M , whose output domain S consists of all possibly aligned models, will
227
      satisfy ϵ-preference privacy if :
228
229                                     P[M (D) ∈ S] ≤ eϵ · P[M (D′ ) ∈ S] + δ.                                     (4)
230
231   From Preference-level DP to Labeler-level DP: Preference-level DP would be a strong guarantee
232
      for each labeler if each labeler (user) labels only one prompt’s responses. However, in practice this
      is not the case, and typically each labeler can annotate multiple responses. In this case labeler-
233
      level privacy safeguards all instances from the same user (labeler). In this scenario, the notion of
234
      adjacency of datasets would be defined w.r.t. presence/absence of a labeler. This distinction has
235   been widely noted and studied in the literature, including including (McMahan et al., 2017; Liu
236   et al., 2020; Levy et al., 2021). One can use techniques from privacy accounting and composition
237   techniques to convert preference-level DP guarantees to labeler-level DP. For the remainder of this
238   paper, we focus on the design of preference-level DP techniques for alignment as the transformation
239   to user level DP can be done by above techniques.
240
      Randomized Response Based Approach: As a first baseline solution, one can achieve ϵ-Preference
241   level DP by Randomized Response (RR). For each entry of the preference dataset D, {x, y1 , y2 , ℓ∗ },
242   the output of the RR mechanism is {x, y1 , y2 , ℓRR }, where:
243
                                                                               eϵ
                                      ∗
244                                    ℓ         with probability (1 − γϵ ) = 1+e ϵ
                              ℓRR =         ∗                             1                            (5)
245                                          1−ℓ       with probability γϵ = 1+eϵ .
246
247   More generally, for a private label generated with RR, ℓRR , the private loss on individual prompt
248
      x ∈ D can be defined as:
249          loss(x, y1 , y2 , lRR ) = 1(lRR = 1) · loss(x, y1 ≻ y2 ) + 1(lRR = 0) · loss(x, y2 ≻ y1 ).             (6)
250
      From the above equation, the expected DPO loss with randomized response can be written as:
251
252          EℓRR [loss(x, y1 , y2 , ℓRR )] =P(ℓRR = ℓ∗ ) · [loss(x, y1 , y2 , lRR |lRR = ℓ∗ )]+
253                                          P(ℓRR ̸= ℓ∗ ) · [loss(x, y1 , y2 , ℓRR |lRR ̸= ℓ∗ )]
254                                         =(1 − γϵ ) · loss[x, y1 , y2 , ℓ∗ ] + γϵ · loss[x, y1 , y2 , 1 − ℓ∗ ]   (7)
255
256   Thus, we observe that the DPO loss on the private preference is a biased estimate of the non-private
257
      DPO loss. To mitigate this issue, we can instead define a new loss function for DPO training as
      follows:
258                                     (
                                            1
259                                             ((1 − γϵ )loss(x, y1 ≻ y2 ) − γϵ loss(x, y2 ≻ y1 )), if ℓRR = 1,
      lossunbiased (x, y1 , y2 , ℓRR ) = 1−2γ
                                            1
                                              ϵ
                                                                                                                           .
260
                                          1−2γϵ ((1 − γϵ )loss(x, y2 ≻ y1 ) − γϵ loss(x, y1 ≻ y2 ), if ℓRR = 0
261                                                                                                         (8)
262
263   We note that the above loss function is an unbiased estimate of the true DPO loss (defined w.r.t. the
264   ground truth human preferences), i.e., E[lossunbiased (x, y1 , y2 , lRR )] = loss(x, yp ≻ ynp ) . This
265   result was shown in Ghazi et al. (2021b).
266   Remark on Connections to & Differences from robust DPO: We note that the problem of preference-
267   level privacy is similar to the problem of robust alignment in presence of noisy preferences. Specif-
268   ically, recent works, including Mitchell (2023), Chowdhury et al. (2024a) and Chowdhury et al.
269   (2024b) study the robustness of alignment when the human-annotated labels are intrinsically noisy.
      The distinction however, is the following: in our setting, the injected noise (and more importantly


                                                              5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      the parameters of the privacy preserving mechanisms (detailed in the next Section)) are known and
271   can be controlled as a function of the privacy parameters. Furthermore, we remark here that some
272   of the theoretical results obtained in Chowdhury et al. (2024a) can be readily applied to our problem
273   and assess the utility-privacy tradeoff of RR based methods. To avoid repetition, we choose not to
274   repeat these kind of results in this paper.
275
276
      3   P ROGRESSIVELY P RIVATE S ELF -A LIGNMENT (PROPS)
277
278
      In this section, we present Progressively Private Self-Alignment algorithm (PROPS), which is the
      main technical contribution of this paper. To facilitate understanding, we first describe PROPS in
279
      a two-stage (K = 2) setting and the generalization to arbitrary number of stages is straightfor-
280
      ward. We begin with the preference dataset D consists of n samples. Each sample is represented
281   as (x, y1 , y2 , ℓ∗ ), where x is the prompt, (y1 , y2 ) are the large language model (LLM) generated re-
282   sponses, and ℓ∗ is the human labeler’s preference. We partition this dataset into two halves, denoted
283   as D1 and D2 . Let us perturb the labels of each entry using the RR mechanism, i.e., the labels are
284   flipped a probability γϵ = 1/(1 + eϵ ).
285
      Stage 1: In the first stage, we use the dataset D1 (with perturbed labels) and use it to align a fine-
286
      tuned model via DPO. Let us denote the resulting model as M1 . First note that since the training
287
      was done on private (perturbed preferences), due to post-processing the model M1 can be used in
288   subsequent stages without additional leakage.
289
290   Stage 2: In this stage, we use the dataset D2 , and use the model M1 (of the pre-
291   vious stage) to label/rank the preference of each prompt/response-pairs.                        Note that
292
      this procedure only requires the prompt and response pairs (and not the ground-
      truth human preferences); thus, this does not cause any additional privacy leakage.
293
      Let us denote the corresponding label obtained from the
294
      model M1 for a prompt as ℓM1 . To summarize, at                           PROPS with K=2 stages
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            <latexit sha1_base64="owqBIfjKnsTo0y3hc3RwaaRtIoU="></latexit>

                                                                                                            M

306   it is not too difficult to work out the MLE combiner using two noisy observations. Assuming that the
307   RR noise and the noise induced by the model M1 are independent, the MLE statistic (log-likelihood
308   ratio) can be written and simplified as follows (see Proof in the Section 6):

323   Estimating γM1 : Now, the remaining challenge is to estimate γM1 , the rate at which the model M1
      predicts incorrect labels. We next present an interesting approach to estimate γM1 . Suppose that


                                                         6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324
      we make the hypothesis that the model M1 also independently flips the ground truth labels with
325   probability γM1 , then we can write the output of the model and the output of the RR mechanisms as
326   follows:
327
328                                    ℓRR = ℓ∗ ⊕ U,          ℓM1 = ℓ∗ ⊕ V,                           (12)
329   where U ∼ Bernoulli(γϵ ) and V ∼ Bernoulli(γM1 ). Thus, estimating γM1 is equivalent to estimat-
330   ing the parameter of the Bernoulli random variable V . Note that we have |D2 | such observations
331   (one for each sample in the second half of the dataset). If we compute
332                                                 P (i)         (i)
                                                        ℓ ⊕ ℓRR
333                                        µM1 = i M1                 ,                           (13)
334                                                       |D2 |
335   which represents the number of disagreements between the labels predicted by RR and the model
336   M1 , this value in fact converges to the the expected value of E[U ⊕V ] = γM1 (1−γϵ )+γϵ (1−γM1 )).
337   Since we know γϵ , we can then use it to compute the unknown parameter γM1 . This leads us to
338   propose the following estimator for γM1 :
339
                                                                   µM1 − γϵ
340                                  (Estimate of γM1 ) γ̂M1 =              .                         (14)
                                                                    1 − 2γϵ
341
342   The detailed proof of the above result and the fact that above estimator is unbiased is presented in
343   the Section 6. With all the above, we have all the ingredients for PROPS.
344
345   Algorithm 1 PROgressively Private Self-alignment (PROPS)
346     Inputs: Fine-tuned Model M0 , Dataset D, Stages K, Privacy parameters (ϵ, δ)
347     Output: Aligned model MK
                                                RR(γϵ )
348     Perform RR on dataset D, such that D −−−−−→ D′
349     Partition the dataset D′ into K-disjoint datasets such that D′ = D1 ∪ D2 ∪ . . . ∪ DK
                                                                              1
350     Flip labels using Randomized Response (RR) with probability γϵ = 1+e    ϵ
351     Align model M0 with dataset D1 to get model M1
352     for k = 2, 3, . . . , K do
353        Generate labels ℓD  Mk−1 for dataset Dk using the model Mk−1 (from previous stage)
                                 k


354        Obtain ℓD        Dk
                   RR and ℓMk−1 and obtain the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) Λ according to
                     k

355        equation 15 and generate label as:
356
357                                                     1 If Λ ≤ 0,
                                            ℓD  k
                                              PROPS =
358                                                     0 If Λ > 0.
359
360       Align model Mk−1 on dataset Dk with PROPS labels (ℓD
                                                             PROPS ) to get model Mk
                                                               k


361     end for
362     Output: Aligned model MK on dataset D
363
364   PROPS Algorithm & Remarks We present the main algorithm of this paper (PROgressively Pri-
365   vate Self-alignment) PROPS in Algorithm 1. We now make a sequence of remarks regarding this
366   algorithm: (a) Privacy guarantees of PROPS: PROPS satisfies (ϵ, 0)-preference-level differential
367   privacy (DP). Furthermore, techniques such as sub-sampling can be readily combined with our idea
368   for privacy amplification. The key aspect of PROPS is that even though it does not explicitly re-
369
      duce privacy leakage, for the same leakage as one would achieve by a vanilla RR mechanism, it can
      potentially provide higher utility (as PROPS trains on potentially less noisy preferences compared
370
      to vanilla RR). (b) Compatibility with RLHF and other alignment approaches: While we have pre-
371
      sented the idea with DPO in the backdrop, the exact same procedure is applicable for alignment using
372   RLHF based algorithms (as these algorithms also require labeled prompt-response pairs). (c) The
373   description of the algorithm satisfies (ϵ, 0)-preference level DP. However, we can lift the algorithm
374   to achieve user/labeler level privacy as discussed in Remark in Section 2. Specifically, achieving
375   labeler level DP would require a bound on the maximum number of responses (per labeler), which
376   can then be used together with composition techniques to provide labeler-level DP guarantees. (d)
377   Distinction from Multi-Stage RR: We would also like to note that even though PROPS bears simi-
      larities to Multi-Stage RR Ghazi et al. (2021b), it has important differences. In multi-stage RR, in


                                                          7
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


378
      each stage the labels for the next stage are obtained by either using RR or via the model from the
379   previous stage (e.g., using a sampling strategy). The distinction lies in the fact how one combines
380   the noisy labels and the model predictions. Instead of using a simple approach of randomly selecting
381   between the two, we instead first estimate the error rate of the model, which are then combined using
382   an MLE approach. Secondly, we note that in LLM alignment, the models are not directly trained
383   for preference classification, but rather for responding to prompts in a socially acceptable manner.
384   The versatility of LLMs and their ability to handle multiple tasks allows us to use the LLMs for
385   dual purposes; namely preference classification for self-alignment. (e) Distinction between PATE
386   and PROPS: We highlight key distinctions between PATE Papernot et al. (2018) and PROPS. First,
387
      PATE provides the privacy of all features; whereas in this paper we focused on preference-level-
      privacy. Second, PROPS is based on a sequential and iterative approach. In each iteration (stage),
388
      we use the model learned from the previous stage to rank the prompt/responses and combine them
389
      with the RR-perturbed rankings. These combined rankings (which are obtained using a Maximum-
390   Likelihood-combiner) are then used to train the model in the next iteration using DPO. On the other
391   hand, PATE follows a parallel-training paradigm; namely it trains multiple teacher models on data
392   partitions in parallel, whose outputs (labels) are then aggregated with DP. These labels are then used
393   for subsequent training. (f) Impact of number of stages and privacy budget in PROPS: The number
394   of stages in the PROPS framework can be treated as a hyperparameter and optimized based on the
395   dataset. Since the 2-stage PROPS approach was already outperforming baseline methods, we did
396   not extend our comparisons to multi-stage settings in our experiments. However, a carefully chosen
397   number of stages has the potential to further enhance the framework’s privacy-utility tradeoff.
398
      4   E XPERIMENTAL S ETUP AND R ESULTS
399
400
      In this Section, we present results on ϵ-preference level DP for DPO, and compare the re-
      sults with our proposed algorithm PROPS. The code for PROPS along with the used datasets
401
      are publicly available . In our experiments and validation, we have used (1) two datasets
402
      (jondurbin/truthy-dpo-v0.1 dataset and Anthropic HH-RLHF ) and (2) three different
403   models of varying sizes: Pythia-1B EleutherAI (2024) , GPT2-Large (774M) OpenAI (2024a) and
404   GPT2-Medium (355M) OpenAI (2024b). We have adopted a similar experimental setup as the prior
405   works Rafailov et al. (2024); Chakraborty et al. (2024); von Werra et al. (2020)Lior-Baruch (2024)
406   Our results are organized as follows: (a) We first show how RR-based scheme and our method
407   (PROPS) impact the Win-Tie-Lose rate relative to a SFT model, and the improvement provided by
408   PROPS compared to RR. (b) We also compare PROPS vs DP-SGD for the three models and the two
409   datasets to show the consistent advantage of PROPS.
410   Impact of Preference Privacy on Win-rate Performance. We now present results using
411   the PROPS algorithm. We implemented a two-stage (K = 2) procedure, where the first
412   stage aligns the initial SFT model using noisy RR perturbed labels to obtain a model M1 ;
413   in the second stage, we use model M1 ’s predictions together with noisy labels (of the sec-
414   ond half of the dataset) to obtain new PROPS labels. We then train the model M1 using
415   PROPS labels to obtain the final model M2 . Figure 4 reports the win-rate for the privately
416   trained DPO models with respect to the fine-tuned model (denoted as SFT for supervised fine-
417
      tuning) used before DPO assuming the truthy-dpo-v0.1 dataset and GPT-2-Large model.
      The objective of this experiment is to observe if the pri-
418
      vately trained DPO models can still attain a compara-
419
      ble utility while enforcing a stronger privacy guaran-
420   tee. Specifically, Figure 4 shows two sets of win-tie-loss
421   rate results: PROPS against SFT and RR against SFT.
422   We report results in the practically relevant high-privacy
423   regime, evaluating Label-DP for four values of ϵ: 0.1, 0.5
424   1, 2. The results indicate that the privately trained meth- Figure 4: Relative performance of RR and
                                                                     PROPS based alignment versus SFT model.
425   ods can still generate responses aligned with societal val-
426   ues compared to their original SFT counterparts while guaranteeing preference DP. As expected, for
427   stricter privacy budgets (i.e. ϵ = 0.1, 0.5) the win-rate is smaller compared to more relaxed privacy
428
      budgets (i.e. ϵ = 1, 2).
429   PROPS vs RR: We report results comparing the responses of PROPS vs. RR mechanism in Fig-
430   ure 5 in terms of Win-Tie-Lose rate. We used GPT-4 as the evaluator and truthy-dpo-v0.1
431   dataset was used for alignment. To analyze the impact of model size, we ran this comparison for
      Pythia-1B, GPT2-Large, and GPT2-Medium models on the truthy-dpo-v0.1 dataset


                                                        8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
      comparing our PROPS method with randomized response (RR) in Figure 5. The results indicate
433   that PROPS can significantly outperform RR when using a larger model such as Pythia-1B.
434   Furthermore, PROPS can benefit larger models more con-
435   sistently (i.e. across varying privacy budgets) more than
436   RR. For Pythia-1B, PROPS beats RR at all privacy
437   budgets and for GPT2-Large (774 million parameters)
438   beats RR at all budgets except ϵ = 0.5, where it loses by
439   a slight margin. For GPT2-Medium (355 million pa-
440   rameters), an interesting trend is observed where PROPS
441
      beats RR at ϵ = 0.1, 2 but loses at ϵ = 0.5, 1. This could Figure 5: Comparison of Win-Tie-Lose
      be a result of GPT2-Medium having fewer parameters rate for PROPS vs RR for high privacy
442                                                              regimes with three models: Pythia, GPT2-
      than GPT2-Large and Pythia, as it would have lower Large, GPT2-Medium.
443
      generalization capabilities causing it to make incorrect
444   predictions during the subsequent stages of PROPS.
445
446   PROPS vs DPSGD: In Figure 6, we present Win-Tie-Lose rates comparing
447
      our proposed algorithm PROPS with the conventional DP-SGD algorithm for
      GPT2-Large and GPT2-Medium models on the truthy-dpo-v0.1 dataset.
448
      DP-SGD was ran using the Gaussian mechanism for 1
449
      epoch with δ = 10−10 , a gradient clipping threshold of
450   10, and a batch size of 4. PROPS was ran for 2 epochs
451   with a batch size of 4. As the results indicate, PROPS
452   is able to consistently outperform DP-SGD at higher pri-
453   vacy regimes (ϵ = 0.1, 0.5, 1) for both models. This indi-
454   cates that while DP-SGD attempts to additionally protect
455   the prompts and responses, it suffers a significant drop in
456   utility for smaller privacy budgets. One critical distinc- Figure 6: Comparison of Win-Tie-Lose
457   tion to highlight is that the implementation of PROPS en- rate for PROPS vs DP-SGD for high privacy
458
      sures a pure differential privacy (DP) guarantee, specifi- regimes with GPT2-Large, GPT2-Medium
      cally (ϵ, 0)-DP. In contrast, DP-SGD provides an approx- models.
459
      imate differential privacy guarantee, denoted as (ϵ, δ)-DP. Next, we present results to show the
460
      mean and standard deviation of the normalized Win-Tie-Lose rates for PROPS vs DP-SGD on (a)
461   HH-RLHF dataset and (b) truthy-dpo-v0.1 datasets in Figure 7 for four privacy parameters.
462   The table indicates that PROPS on-average outperforms DP-SGD on both datasets at high privacy
463   regimes (details regarding the experiments are presented in the Appendix).
464
465
466
467
468
469
470
471   Figure 7: Mean and Standard Deviation for 5 independent runs on truthy-dpo-v0.1, and HH-RLHF
472   datasets for high-privacy and moderate-privacy regimes with GPT2-Medium model.
473   Illustrative Example Responses to Prompts for varying privacy levels: To further illustrate the
474   privacy-utitity tradeoff, we analyze the responses of LLMs trained at different privacy budgets for
475   the same prompt. Figure 8 presents responses for models trained when ϵ = 0, 1, ∞ for two different
476   prompts. For instance, the first prompt is: “I’ve been seeing a lot of slugs outside recently, even
477
      crawling up trees. Should I do something about them, or just let them be?”. As the table indicates,
      the model trained with ϵ = 0 (corresponding to random flipping) only regurgitates the question to
478
      the user. The model trained with ϵ = 1 does produce an actual response but introduces its own
479
      opinion/bias on the matter. The model trained with ϵ = ∞ gives a more “professional” answer but
480   is not very helpful. For the second prompt, an interesting trend can be observed where the models
481   trained when ϵ = 0, ∞ behave similarly to the previous example, but at ϵ = 1 a more informa-
482   tive answer is given. This again indicates that privacy can be ensured while still attaining a decent
483   utility. It should be noted that quality assessment of language models is inherently subjective and
484   could alternatively be analyzed along three individual axes: (a) Coherence, (b) Helpfulness, and (c)
485   Harmlessness. While GPT-4 can provide fine-grained rankings for individual attributes, combining
      these into an overall judgment remains subjective. When generating the win-tie-loss results in the

515   nesses and individuals?” for GPT2-Large trained with PROPS at ϵ = 0.1 and when ϵ = ∞ (i.e. non-private).
516
517
      main paper, we focused on prompting GPT-4 to equally prioritize coherence, helpfulness and harm-
      fulness when evaluating responses. We did this so that we could assess the win-tie-lose statistics
518
      for each model across a large sample size of prompts. Having said that, if we were to assess each
519
      response individually one can in principle ask GPT-4 to provide fine-grained rankings for each at-
520   tribute (coherence, helpfulness, harmlessness). However, combining these rankings to rate or judge
521   a response as superior becomes subjective. As an example we show these fine-grained rankings for
522   two responses from the same prompt at different privacy budgets in Figure 9 under varying privacy
523   budgets for GPT2-Large.
524
525
      5    C ONCLUSIONS
526
527   In this paper, we presented new results towards aligning LLMs with preference level privacy, which
528   preserve the privacy of preferences provided by humans. We build and expand upon the concept of
529
      label DP for this problem, and present a series of increasingly sophisticated, yet practical privacy
      preserving mechanisms for alignment. Specifically, starting from a standard randomized response
530
      (RR) mechanism which randomly flips human preferences, we presented a new mechanism, PROPS
531
      (PROgressively Private Self-alignment) which works across multiple stages. The key insight behind
532   PROPS is that while intermediate LLM models may not yet be fully capable of generating high-
533   quality outputs or responses in the early stages of training, it may still possess sufficient knowledge
534   to correctly label preferences. Thus, our framework leverages the power of intermediate models to
535   enhance alignment efficiency while preserving privacy, offering a novel solution to the challenge of
536   privacy-preserving alignment. We also provided a comprehensive set of experiments on two datasets
537   and multiple model sizes which show that PROPS outperforms DP-SGD and randomized response
538   (RR) based approaches. We quantified and measure these gains in terms of win-tie-lose rates, and
539   these gains are especially substantial in practically relevant high privacy regimes.

      6     APPENDIX
649
650   6.1   MLE ESTIMATOR FOR ℓ∗ USING (ℓRR , ℓM1 ): EQUATION 10
651
652
      Given the flipped labels ℓRR and ℓM1 by Randomized Response (RR) and model M1 respectively,
      we aim to come up with a good decision making policy for the proposed algorithm. We calculate
653
      the likelihood of observing (ℓRR , ℓM1 ) given the possible values of ℓ∗ . We define γϵ as the flipping
654
      probability of RR and γM1 as the flipping probability of the model.
655
656
657
658
659
660   Figure 10: The table represents the probability of observing ℓRR and ℓM based on the flipping
661   probabilities γϵ and γM1 and true label ℓ∗ .
662
      For binary ℓ∗ , now we present the probability of observing specific values of ℓRR and ℓM1 . To find
663   the best estimator, we compute the log-likelihood ratio:
664
                                      P(ℓRR , ℓM1 | ℓ∗ = 0)

665                        Λ = log                                                                    (15)
666                                   P(ℓRR , ℓM1 | ℓ∗ = 1)
                                      P(ℓRR |ℓ∗ = 0) · P(ℓM1 |ℓ∗ = 0)

667                          (a)
                              = log
668                                   P(ℓRR |ℓ∗ = 1) · P(ℓM1 |ℓ∗ = 1)
669
                                                1 − γϵ                     1 − γM1

                              (b)    ℓRR                        ℓ M1
670                           = (−1)      log            + (−1)      log
                                                  γϵ                         γM
671
672   where, (a) is obtained since ℓRR and ℓM1 are independent, and (b) values are obtained from Table
673   10. This concludes the proof of equation 10.
674
675   6.2   E STIMATOR FOR γM1 : P ROOF OF EQUATION 14
676
677   We have noisy labels ℓRR generated by RR with a flipping probability of γϵ , and predicted labels
678
      ℓM1 by the model M1 with a flipping (error) probability of γM1 . We define ℓRR and ℓM1 as:
679                                     ℓRR = ℓ∗ ⊕ U,        ℓM1 = ℓ∗ ⊕ V,                              (16)
680
681   where U ∼ Bernoulli(γϵ ) and V ∼ Bernoulli(γM1 ). We first make the observation that for ith
                              (i)   (i)
682   sample in the dataset, ℓM1 ⊕ ℓRR = (ℓ∗i ⊕ Vi ) ⊕ (ℓ∗i ⊕ Vi ) = Vi ⊕ Ui , where, Ui and Vi are
683   independent. Now, define
684                                             P (i)        (i)
685                                                i ℓM1 ⊕ ℓRR
                                        µM1 =
686                                                   |D2 |
687   and note that µM1 is an unbiased estimator for the expected value of E[U ⊕ V ] = γM1 (1 − γϵ ) +
688   γϵ (1 − γM1 )). Hence, we can use µM to obtain an estimate for γ̂M1 as follows:
689
690                                                     (µM1 − γϵ )
                                               γ̂M1 =                                                   (17)
691                                                       1 − 2γϵ
692   This concludes the proof of equation 14.

702
      6.3      T RAINING D ETAILS
703
704   We now summarize how the models were trained for the experiments. The training procedures for
705   each dataset were as follows:
706
      truthy-dpo-v0.1: 15% of the data was used for SFT. 75% of the data was used for passing
707
      the model through DPO. This segment of data was split in half, and two epochs of DPO were
708   ran over each half. We then filtered the dataset to keep the preference pairs that were generated
709   by prompting an LLM to be “an honest and helpful assistant” and performed DPO using half of
710   the dataset for 3 epochs. The Win-Tie-Loss rates are calculated using the remaining 10% of the
711   truthy-dpo-v0.1 dataset, which is equivalent to 100 prompts.
712
      HH-RLHF: We use an SFT model1 available on huggingface that was trained for 1 epoch over the
713
      Anthropic-HH dataset. 1000 samples from the test set were used to run DPO. Specifically, these
714   samples were split into 2 halves, and DPO was run for three epochs on each half. While the same
715   prompts were run for both PROPS and DP-SGD, PROPS was fed prompts from the same dataset but
716   with a different format2 . 100 samples from the same test set (see Footnote 2) were used to generate
717   the Win-Tie-Loss results.
